figures.caption: Fig. 2: Overview of AA-Splat. After unprojecting per-view depth maps to obtain the 3D Gaussian centers, the maximal frequency bound of each 3D Gaussian primitive is computed from context view camera poses and focal lengths, which is then converted to its 3D band-limiting Gaussian post-filter. The GS head predicts the residual Gaussian parameters which are then post-filtered by the 3D filters to obtain the bandlimited 3D Gaussians. During rendering, opacity balancing (OB) compensates for the increase in overlap between pixel-aligned Gaussian primitives for improved photometric supervision and rendering fidelity.

Fig. 3: Multi-scale visual comparison on RE10K [45]. ×4 indicates rendering target views at higher resolution to simulate zoom-in effects, while ×1/4 indicates rendering target views at lower resolution to simulate zoom-out effects. Additional visualizations will be available in supplementary materials.

Fig. 1: Ablation results on the DL3DV [24] dataset. All variants perform feedforward reconstruction with 2 context views of 256 × 448 resolution and are rendered at 4× resolution to mimic zoom-in effects. Removing either OB or 3D-BLPF causes unnatural grid-like artifacts or sharp jagged edges. Using only the 2D Mip filter also causes grid-like artifacts. Our method (AA-Splat) effectively suppresses these rendering artifacts, enabling smooth interpolation. Best viewed zoomed-in.

Fig. 4: Multi-scale visual comparison of cross-dataset generalization on ACID [25]. Models trained on RE10K are directly used to test on scenes from ACID. Additional visualizations will be available in supplementary materials.
